ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301099 covers milk tea as a category; no specific code exists for honey-flavoured fresh milk tea variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific facet code for honey milk tea exists in the published matrix. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for honey milk tea. No code assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard takeaway cup of fresh honey milk tea, approximately 400 ml / 400 g, as typically served at Taiwanese-style tea shops in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Milk tea, prepared with whole milk and sugar — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; supplemented with honey composition from USDA FoodData Central NDB 19296
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for fresh honey milk tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining: (1) USDA FoodData Central entry for tea with milk (approximately 60 ml brewed black tea + 40 ml whole milk per 100 ml serving ratio), and (2) honey contribution (USDA NDB 19296, Honey) at approximately 8 g per 100 ml of finished beverage. Macros are weighted-average estimates. Energy calculated from Atwater factors. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; honey GI reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008, Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fresh honey milk tea. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on: (1) honey GI of approximately 55-58 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which is the primary glycemic driver; (2) whole milk GI of approximately 27–34 (low), which moderates the overall GI; (3) black tea contributes negligible carbohydrate. Weighted composite GI of 55 is a conservative low estimate. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (55 × 43.2 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 23.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~22 mg per 100 g; ~88 mg per 400 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Fresh honey milk tea is low in sodium and does not pose a significant hypertension risk when consumed occasionally, though individuals on strict fluid or calorie restrictions should account for the added honey sugar content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content from honey (~10.2 g sugar per 100 g; ~40.8 g per 400 g serving) with a low glycemic index of 55
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid fresh honey milk tea due to its substantial free sugar load from honey, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 40 g of sugar and a glycemic load of approximately 24.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from whole milk (~0.9 g per 100 g; ~3.6 g per 400 g serving) and high free sugar from honey
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should consume fresh honey milk tea in moderation, as the combination of saturated fat from whole milk and high free sugar from honey may contribute to dyslipidaemia and elevated triglycerides with frequent consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fresh Honey Milk Tea (鲜奶蜜茶 / 鮮奶蜜茶; pinyin: Xiān Nǎi Mì Chá) is a Taiwanese-origin milk tea beverage made by combining freshly brewed black tea or oolong tea with fresh whole milk and natural honey as the primary sweetener, in place of the refined sugar or syrup used in conventional bubble tea. The drink is associated with premium Taiwanese tea-shop chains such as CHICHA San Chen, which operates in Singapore. The beverage is served chilled or at room temperature in a standard 400 ml takeaway cup. Its flavour profile is mildly sweet with floral honey notes, a creamy dairy body from the fresh milk, and a subtle astringency from the tea base. The texture is smooth and liquid, without tapioca pearls unless specified. Nutritionally, a 400 g serving provides approximately 248 kcal, 6.4 g protein, 5.6 g fat, and 43.2 g carbohydrate, of which approximately 40.8 g is sugar derived primarily from honey. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 24 per serving, making it a significant source of free sugars. Clinically, this beverage is of concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high free sugar content, and should be consumed in moderation by those managing cardiovascular disease. It is low in sodium and suitable for most hypertensive individuals when consumed occasionally. The drink is vegetarian, halal-friendly, and gluten-free, but contains dairy and is not suitable for vegans or those with lactose intolerance.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for fresh honey milk tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for tea with whole milk and honey (USDA NDB 19296) in proportions consistent with a standard Taiwanese-style honey milk tea recipe. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database honey reference (GI ~55) and whole milk reference (GI ~30), weighted by carbohydrate contribution. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for tea with whole milk and honey; no single approved-source entry for this exact product exists. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): tea, milk, and honey contribute negligible dietary fibre; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central whole milk data; trace ruminant trans fats below reporting threshold). 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite used for this estimate. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite used for this estimate. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite used for this estimate. 7. GI set to 55 (low) based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database honey reference and whole milk reference; no direct GI measurement for this product exists. 8. Serving size of 400 g is based on standard Taiwanese tea-shop takeaway cup volume (approximately 400 ml). 9. Price data (SGD 7.80) preserved from field survey data source record for reference only; not used as a nutritional evidence source. 10. Multilingual names for Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are translations of the canonical English name, as no established local-language canonical names exist for this Taiwanese-origin product in Singapore.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full record from USDA FoodData Central composite estimate. Identified that dietary_fibre_g=0.0 and trans_fat_g=0.0 required zero-justification documentation in notes. Initial gi_category for GI 55 was incorrectly set to 'medium'.

Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for glycemic_index 55, aligning with University of Sydney GI Database classification. Added explicit zero-justification phrases for dietary_fibre_g and trans_fat_g in notes. Added -1 sentinel documentation for vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, and selenium_mcg. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases. Confirmed all hard gates pass and score is 8/8.
